summary:
The French and Indian War (1754–1763 AD) was the North American theater of the [[event:2332|Seven Years' War]] — the first major conflict that began in the Americas before spreading to Europe — in which Britain and its colonial and Native American allies defeated France and its Native allies for control of eastern North America, expelling France from Canada and establishing British territorial dominance from the Atlantic to the Mississippi. The war began in the Ohio Valley, where both British colonial interests and the French were competing for control of the strategic forks of the Ohio River (modern Pittsburgh); a young Virginia militia officer named George Washington led an expedition that ambushed a French scouting party in 1754, triggering the conflict.

The war's early years were disastrous for the British: General Braddock's expedition to capture Fort Duquesne was ambushed and destroyed (1755), with Washington narrowly escaping; British forts on the Great Lakes frontier fell. The turning point came when William Pitt the Elder became Britain's wartime minister and poured resources into the North American theater. The fall of Louisbourg (1758), Fort Duquesne (1758), and most decisively, Quebec (1759) — where both commanding generals Wolfe (British) and Montcalm (French) died in the Battle of the Plains of Abraham — gave Britain effective control of Canada. The [[event:2393|Treaty of Paris]] (1763) confirmed France's expulsion from North America. The war's consequence for the [[event:106|American Revolution]] was profound: it eliminated the French threat that had made the colonies dependent on British protection, removed the strategic rationale for the colonial relationship, and left Britain with enormous war debts that it tried to recover through the colonial taxes that triggered revolution.
